posts.title: Your Week Ahead in Warrenville: June 17-23
posts.author: Warrenville Proud
posts.excerpt: Movies in the park, live lunchtime music, and the first signs of Fourth of July season. Here is what is happening around town this week.
posts.body_html:
Summer is in full swing in Warrenville, and this week brings a couple of easy, free ways to get out of the house and enjoy it. Here is what we have our eye on.

Thursday: Summer Movies in the Park

The Park District's outdoor movie series continues this week. Pack a blanket and some snacks and settle in for a film under the stars. It is free, it is family friendly, and it is the kind of low-key summer night that makes a town feel like home. Check the Park District events page for the title and start time.

All week: Lunchtime Live

If your schedule allows a real lunch break, the Lunchtime Live music series brings a little energy to the middle of the day downtown. A nice reason to grab food from a local spot and actually sit outside for a bit.

Looking ahead: the Fourth is coming

It is not this week, but it is close enough to start planning. Warrenville's Fourth of July celebration runs July 3 and 4, with the parade on the 3rd and the Firecracker 5K on the morning of the 4th. If you run, registration tends to fill up, so keep an eye on the city's community events page.

That is the week. Get outside, support a local spot or two, and we will see you around town.

posts.title: 5 Free Things to Do in Warrenville This Summer
posts.author: Warrenville Proud
posts.excerpt: You do not need to leave town or spend a dime to have a great summer here. Five favorites, from movies in the park to the best weekend of the year.
posts.body_html:
One of the quietly great things about Warrenville is how much there is to do without spending money or driving far. Here are five free ways to make the most of summer right here at home.

1. Movies in the Park

The Park District runs outdoor movie nights through the summer. Bring a blanket, bring the kids, and enjoy a film as the sun goes down. Hard to beat for a free family night.

2. Lunchtime Live

Live music downtown in the middle of the day. Grab lunch from a local spot and enjoy the show. A small thing that makes a regular weekday feel a little more like summer.

3. The Illinois Prairie Path

Warrenville sits right on one of the best trails in the region. Walk it, run it, or bike it. The stretch through Warrenville Grove Forest Preserve is especially pretty in the morning.

4. The Fourth of July Celebration

The parade on July 3 and the festivities through the Fourth are a Warrenville highlight, and watching is free. Line up early for a good parade spot.

5. Summer Daze

The big one. Warrenville's signature festival lands August 7 and 8 at the City Hall complex, with live music, a car show, food, and local business booths. Admission to wander and soak it in costs nothing, and it is the weekend the whole town shows up.

Save this list, share it with a neighbor, and get out there. Summer in Warrenville goes fast.

posts.title: A Walker's Guide to St. James Farm, Warrenville's Quiet Gem
posts.author: Warrenville Proud
posts.excerpt: An easy two-mile loop through a former family estate, complete with old stone buildings, big open meadows, and a little surprise art along the way. Here is how to make the most of it.
posts.body_html:
Ask folks around town for their favorite local walk and a lot of them will point you toward the Prairie Path. But just a few minutes away sits one of the most peaceful spots in the area, and plenty of newer neighbors have never set foot on it. St. James Farm Forest Preserve is a quiet stretch of woods, prairie, and history on Warrenville's western edge, and it makes for one of the nicest easy outings in DuPage County. Here is what to know before you go.

A little history under your feet

St. James Farm started as a private retreat for the McCormick family back in 1920. Over the years it grew into a working farm known for its Guernsey cattle and, maybe more memorably, for hosting horse competitions and an annual steeplechase that drew big crowds. The Forest Preserve District of DuPage County now keeps it open to everyone, and the old riding arena, historic barns, and scattered artwork are still here to wander past. You get the rare feeling of walking through a piece of local history that has been handed back to the town.

The trail itself

The main path is a crushed stone loop of about two miles. It is flat, friendly, and easy to follow, which makes it a good pick for a relaxed family walk, a morning jog, or a slow stroll with a camera. The loop rolls past woodlands, restored prairie, and open meadow, so there is always something a little different around the next bend. If you want to keep going, the trail connects out to the Illinois Prairie Path, the DuPage River trail, and the Herrick Lake trail, so you can stitch together a much longer ride or hike when the mood strikes.

Good for more than walking

The preserve welcomes hikers, bikers, and horseback riders, and in winter it turns into a lovely spot for cross-country skiing. Photographers tend to love it too, between the historic buildings, the meadows at golden hour, and the bits of sculpture you stumble across along the way. It is the kind of place that rewards going slow.

Before you head out

The main entrance is on the east side of Winfield Road, about a quarter mile north of Butterfield Road. The preserve is open from one hour after sunrise to one hour after sunset, and there is no charge to visit. Bring water, especially on warm summer mornings, and wear shoes you do not mind getting a little dusty. As always, it is worth a quick check of the Forest Preserve District site before a trip, since the indoor arena and historic buildings sometimes host special events.

If you have lived here for years and never made the turn onto Winfield, this is your nudge. Pack a water bottle, grab a neighbor, and go see one of the prettiest corners of Warrenville for yourself.
